data.title: Wendy's: The Selloff Has Gone Too Far With A Turnaround Plan Kicking Off
data.description:
The stock market is defying all expectations and hitting fresh new records, despite a resurgence in trade war risks plus a shaky U.S. macroeconomy. In spite of this, now is hardly the time for complacency: in my view, there has never been a better time for investors to rotate more of our portfolios into undervalued rebound plays, even if they have company-specific execution risks on the horizon.

Wendy’s (NASDAQ:WEN) is a stock that caught my radar. The lagging fast food chain has seen its share price slide by roughly half this year, as investors consider the risk of deteriorating U.S. comp sales and margin headwinds. But for discerning investors, Wendy’s could offer a great catalyst-driven rebound play that has low correlation to the rest of the AI-heavy market.

I’m initiating Wendy’s at a buy rating. While it’s true that the company is operating in a difficult macro environment, we look to the company’s menu shake-ups, a new turnaround plan, and relatively stronger overseas performance as core reasons to buy Wendy’s on the dip.

Wendy’s has problems, but none are insurmountable

To get a clean grip on Wendy’s, we should first kick off the discussion with what is going wrong with the company, to understand why the stock has declined so sharply since the start of the year.

Problem number one for Wendy’s is that U.S. same-restaurant sales growth has been lagging all year, which has created headaches for the company and for investors. As a reminder, same-restaurant sales growth (often also called “comps” by other companies in the industry) measures revenue growth from existing locations, excluding the contribution from newly opened or newly closed stores. As shown in the chart below, Wendy’s Q2 comps declined at a -3.6% y/y pace, which decelerated versus -2.6% y/y in Q1.

Competitively, this doesn’t look great for the company. In Q2, McDonald's achieved a return to positive 2.5% U.S. comps:

Certainly however, Wendy’s isn’t alone in facing deteriorating results. Chipotle (CMG), once a growth star in the industry, saw comps deteriorate -360 bps y/y to -4.0% in the same quarter, which is worse than Wendy’s in terms of the nominal decline as well as sequential deceleration.

Whether these results are a result of temporary macro headwinds or a more permanent shift in competitive dynamics remains to be seen. What seems to be evident, however, is that the industry is facing a more promotional environment. McDonald’s achieved its return to positive U.S. sales comps in part by heavily featuring its $5 Meal Deal as a national promotion (even in more pricey markets like California, where fast food chains are subject to a punishing $15 minimum wage) with $1 optional add-ons. McDonald’s has also followed this up with its highly popular Monopoly game promotion running from October to late November (Q4), featuring free food prizes as well as a low chance of higher-tier prizes, including free trips, cars, and cash.

Meanwhile, Wendy’s may not have enough room on the margin front to sustain these promotions to compete. Operating margins for owned U.S. locations (non-franchise) in Q2 fell -30 bps y/y to 16.2%.

Further complicating Wendy’s declining margins and lackluster sales is a mounting debt load. Wendy’s Q2 balance sheet has $2.74 billion of debt on it, or $2.26 billion net debt after factoring in cash. This is a 4.2x multiple of the company’s adjusted EBITDA guidance for the year, well above the ~3x leverage ratio that most commercial banks consider to be highly leveraged already.

A turnaround plan and new menu shake-ups

Now that we’ve covered all of Wendy’s core risks, we’ll say that would be a mistake to think that Wendy’s is taking the losses lying down. Just recently, in early October, the company announced a new turnaround plan called “Project Fresh” aimed at improving customer performance as well as backend operations. The broad strokes of the plan are shown below:

Notable is the fact that Wendy’s has brought on the former CEO of Taco Bell and Yum! (YUM), whose subsidiaries have performed relatively well this year, to consult on Wendy’s issues. While I’m generally skeptical of the ability of management consultants to bring about effective change, the high profile industry expertise of Greg Creed during a time that Wendy’s is still in search of a full-time CEO (its current interim CEO is leading the company while also retaining CFO duties) may help the company drive better strategic decisions. We also like that this plan aims to allocate more capital to reinvest into U.S. restaurants to directly try to influence sagging AUV and turn around negative comps.

Another headline worth watching: in September, Wendy’s launched a new chicken tenders menu item branded “Tendy’s”, an amusing play-on branding that may help to re-energize newness and drive customers back in the door. We don’t have to look farther back than the rising popularity of Popeye’s chicken sandwiches to note that chicken products are highly popular and can bring a sudden re-emergence to the brand (especially amid the high-protein diets that are popular today: Wendy’s may not have much credibility as a health food, but its all-white meat chicken breasts used in the Tendy’s tenders may draw some appeal).

This is one in a string of recent menu shakeups that may potentially help to catalyze new visits. Wendy’s also introduced new drinks, including a new increasingly popular cold brew:

Valuation and key takeaways

Now we come to the crux of why to invest in Wendy’s: as Wendy’s plants the seeds for potential re-acceleration drivers, its valuation sits at market-topping lows amid very low Street expectations as well.

At current share prices near $9, Wendy’s trades at a market cap of $1.72 billion. After we net off the $479.5 million of cash and $2.74 billion of debt on the company’s latest balance sheet, Wendy’s resulting enterprise value is much higher at $3.98 billion.

For FY25, Wendy’s is guiding to -3% to -5% sales growth, which has worsened from an original view of -2% to flat. It’s now expecting $505-525 million of adjusted EBITDA, 4% lower than prior expectations.

This positions Wendy’s at a substantially lower 7.7x EV/FY25 adjusted EBITDA multiple. We find that the gap versus peers like McDonald’s at a high teens multiple is too wide, and we can hardly explain the gap to Chipotle, the most expensive stock in the pack whose comp sales are even worse than Wendy’s.

We have one more potential upside to note: while fixing the U.S. remains Wendy’s core focus, we note that international has remained positive. We can point to a number of other fast food chains, namely Domino’s Pizza (DPZ), which has found success in leaning on franchisee fees overseas to drive growth, despite lagging U.S. results.

All in all, I think pessimism is overblown on Wendy’s. While we certainly can’t be blind to the performance risks this company has shown while it works to execute an uphill turnaround, a ~8x adjusted EBITDA multiple is also too low to ignore in this market. Use the dip as a buying opportunity.

data.title: Eton Pharmaceuticals Has Focused Growth But Valuation Limits Upside: Why I Choose To Hold
data.description:
Eton Pharmaceuticals, Inc. (NASDAQ:ETON) stock has had a great run for the past 52-weeks enjoying triple-digit gains. The company currently stands out as a focused rare-disease innovator. It has lately shown rapid growth and strong execution. The company's expanding portfolio and disciplined management inspire confidence. That said, I think that its valuation looks stretched. Given near-term execution and regulatory risks, I currently assign a HOLD rating on ETON stock.

Understanding Eton's Business Model

Eton's reason of existence is to develop and commercialize treatments for rare diseases. The company generates revenue by selling specialized FDA-approved drugs. These products include ALKINDI SPRINKLE, INCRELEX, GALZIN, and KHINDIVI.

Eton's customers include hospitals, physicians, and specialty pharmacies that serve pediatric and rare-disease patients. In terms of payments, the company's customers pay through standard pharmaceutical channels, often reimbursed by insurance or government programs.

It's important to note that Eton’s revenue is driven by product sales, new drug launches, licensing agreements, and label expansions. The company's pediatric endocrinology and rare-disease portfolio is a high-margin area that has been fueling company growth.

Assessing Eton's Pipeline

Eton is currently developing five new medicines that are focused on rare and pediatric diseases. The one more advanced now is ET-600, an oral treatment for diabetes insipidus. This drug could be approved by early 2026. Most noteworthy, ET-600 recently received a second U.S. patent extending protection through 2044.

Meanwhile, other drugs in progress include Amglidia. This one is for rare metabolic disorders. ET-700 is another one and is described as more of an easier-to-take version of Galzin. Galzin treats Wilson disease by preventing harmful copper buildup in patients.

ET-800 is another one under Eton's pipeline, a late-stage rare disease drug candidate. Meanwhile, another one is the ZENEO hydrocortisone injector, which is meant to offer rapid, needle-free emergency cortisol delivery.

All these products under Eton have one aim. That is, to help patients with serious and often overlooked conditions. Together, they target a market that ‘is expected to come in at $242.5 billion in 2025.’ I believe that this offers Eton strong growth potential if approvals and launches go as planned.

Revenue Drivers: Brief Q2 Update

Eton doesn't provide revenue split by product in detail, but management noted that INCRELEX drove the majority of Q2 2025 growth. This product reportedly performed far above expectations following its relaunch.

That said, ALKINDI SPRINKLE and the newly launched KHINDIVI also continued to strengthen Eton’s adrenal insufficiency franchise.

KHINDIVI is still fresh on the market after its launch in June 2025. This came through shortly after receiving FDA approval on May 28, 2025. The drug treats pediatric adrenal insufficiency.

GALZIN contributed solid gains during the quarter. Like INCRELEX, these gains came through after relaunch. Overall, these four core products generated nearly all of the company’s $18.9 million in Q2 2025 revenue. Most noteworthy, this gain was 108% year-over-year ((YoY)).

Why the Relaunches?

Eton relaunched INCRELEX in early 2025, and this was following its late 2024 acquisition. GALZIN was also brought back to the market in March 2025. Both relaunches were meant to revive underperforming but high-potential rare-disease treatments.

INCRELEX had been suffering because of declining prescriptions over the years. The drug lacked proper awareness among pediatric endocrinologists. The company then came up with active physician outreach, medical conference participation, and improved patient education programs, which are now bearing fruits.

Likewise, GALZIN’s earlier problems of being hard to find in pharmacies and being too expensive for many patients have been fixed. The company did so by its $0 copay program, specialty pharmacy partnerships, and patient-support services.

Eton partnered with specialty pharmacies like Eton Cares and select rare-disease distributors to ensure reliable supply, support, and patient access.

What's interesting is that management now has this renewed commercial focus, enhanced education for its products, coupled with affordability initiatives. I believe that these strategic improvements position both therapies for long-term sustainability. The results so far have been quick, as evidenced by rapid patient growth and strong early adoption trends reported in Q2 2025.

Competition

Eton's main competitors in its space are Ipsen and Neurocrine Biosciences.

Ipsen markets its product, Increlex, internationally and has been continuously expanding its endocrinology franchise. Eton acquired the U.S. rights to Increlex from Ipsen in late 2024. This means that Ipsen remains a competitor only in international markets where it still sells the drug.

This gives Ipsen global presence but less focus on U.S. niche markets. Meanwhile, Eton primarily operates in the United States and Europe.

On the other hand, Neurocrine offers adrenal-insufficiency treatments like Alkindi and Efmody. The products are both gaining traction in Europe and entering the U.S. market.

Eton’s strength has been largely premised on its deep U.S. specialization in pediatric rare diseases, strong patient-support programs and efficient launches unlike these larger rivals. I think that this focused approach and expanding pipeline positions Eton as a growing challenger with probably sustainable long-term potential.

Cash Positioning

As of Q2, Eton held $25.4 million in cash up from $14.9 million at year-end 2024. The company also generated $8 million in operating cash flow. I view the company's burn rate as modest considering its positive cash generation plus limited near-term spending growth.

This provides a comfortable cash runway exceeding 12 months. I think that the company will still be able to support its ongoing product launches, regulatory filings and late-stage pipeline developments without immediate need for external financing.

Key Risks

Eton faces several key risks that could affect its momentum.

One of the most critical risks considering its wide pipeline is regulatory delays. Such delays could slow the approval or label expansion of key drugs like ET-600. On the other hand, I also think that supply chain disruptions have the potential to impact production or timely distribution of its rare-disease medicines.

The other thing is the company's heavy dependence on a small patient population. This is so because of its focus on rare diseases. The implication now is that any form of slower-than-expected adoption could hurt the company's revenues.

Additionally, I also think that any changes in insurance coverage or reimbursement policies could affect patient access. Despite these risks, Eton’s strong cash position and efficient operations provide a buffer for continued progress

Valuation

Eton’s forward EV/EBITDA of 25.04 is roughly double the industry average of 12.51. At the same time, it is far above peers like Cresco Labs (5.3) and Verano Holdings (3.9). I think that this premium is a likely reflection of investor expectations for Eton’s high-margin rare-disease portfolio, consistent revenue growth and expanding profitability. However, I also believe that such a high multiple also implies limited margin for error. Any form of regulatory delay, slower adoption or pipeline setback could pressure valuation. I'm of the view that sustained execution and pipeline success are key to supporting this elevated market confidence.

Conclusion

I view Eton as a company presenting a compelling growth story built on a focused rare-disease portfolio, strong execution and expanding profitability. I'm impressed by the company’s strategic relaunches, disciplined spending, and promising pipeline which is anchored by ET-600. This positions it well for continued momentum. However, given its already premium valuation and execution risks tied to regulatory and adoption timelines, I choose to assign a HOLD rating on ETON stock. I believe that besides Eton being fundamentally strong, waiting for clearer proof of sustained revenue and pipeline-driven earnings before re-rating higher would be a great idea for now.

data.title: EUSA: A Suboptimal Choice With Surprisingly Higher Risk, Lower Reward
data.description:
Perhaps one of the main concerns equity investors have been expressing at least since the pandemic is that the market rally lacks breadth. In other words, there is a small cohort of locomotives that are responsible for the lion's share of the S&P 500's gains, while the rest are either trying to more or less keep pace with them or just languishing. So this creates certain disproportions, resulting in increasing top-heaviness that amplifies the already quite acute valuation issue that, in turn, opens the door for steep drawdowns in corrections and bear markets.

And the answer to that is seemingly simple—it is implementing a weighting scheme that would place equal importance on all the components of an index instead of rewarding the most generously valued companies and pushing them to the very top.

However, the problem I should highlight here is that in practice, while equal weighting does solve the valuation problem to a degree, it does not deliver much in terms of reducing risk. And today, we will be discussing the iShares MSCI USA Equal Weighted ETF (NYSEARCA:EUSA), the passively managed vehicle that has not only underperformed the iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV) since its strategy change in 2015 but also demonstrated surprisingly uncomfortable risk characteristics for a strategy supportive of a lower P/E and lower beta.

So while it might appeal to investors seeking exposure to the market but with more comfortable valuations, I do not think a robust bullish thesis could be constructed for it.

Basics Of The iShares MSCI USA Equal Weighted ETF

  • Inception: May 5, 2010
  • Investment Adviser: BlackRock Fund Advisors
  • AUM: $1.4 billion, as of October 24
  • Expense Ratio: 0.09%
  • 10-Year Annualized Total Return (October 2015–September 2025): 11.62% vs. 15.27% of IVV

EUSA Strategy

As we know from the iShares website, the MSCI USA Equal Weighted Index is the core of EUSA's strategy. However, this has not always been so. Footnotes on page S-76 of the prospectus say that this index

...effective as of September 1, 2015, replaced the MSCI USA Index as the Underlying Index of the Fund.

Considering the small but fundamentally important difference between these indices (that is, their weighting scheme), I believe it is reasonable to ignore EUSA's performance prior to September 2015.

Portfolio Analysis: EUSA vs. IVV

As of October 22, there were 544 equities in the EUSA portfolio, with the main ten accounting for just 2.73% (compare this to 38.74% in IVV), which is no coincidence considering the weighting scheme. Little surprise that the cohort of the key ten names in this basket is almost entirely different (with one exception being Tesla (TSLA)) from those of IVV.

Symbol Weight in EUSA, as of October 22 Weight in IVV, as of October 22 Sector Market Cap, $ billion
Warner Bros. Discovery, Inc. (WBD) 0.31% 0.09% Communication 50.83
MongoDB, Inc. (MDB) 0.29% N/A Information Technology 26.45
Micron Technology, Inc. (MU) 0.29% 0.39% Information Technology 222.78
Western Digital Corporation (WDC) 0.29% 0.07% Information Technology 41.30
Pure Storage, Inc. (PSTG) 0.28% N/A Information Technology 29.22
Intel Corporation (INTC) 0.28% 0.28% Information Technology 175.64
Rocket Lab Corporation (RKLB) 0.26% N/A Industrials 29.31
Seagate Technology Holdings plc (STX) 0.25% 0.08% Information Technology 45.74
AppLovin Corporation (APP) 0.24% 0.27% Information Technology 191.05
Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) 0.24% 2.15% Consumer Discretionary 1,459.64

Created by the author using data from the ETFs and Seeking Alpha. Data as of October 23

As to overlap, while IVV and EUSA have a lot in common, they are certainly not identical, as, for instance, EUSA has exposure to numerous names that did not qualify for the S&P 500, including MercadoLibre, Inc. (MELI), RKLB, etc.

Parameter EUSA IVV
Stocks present in both EUSA and IVV portfolios 84.85% 99.17%

Calculated by the author using data from the ETFs

Sector Mix

The main consequence of EUSA's equal weighting is that industrials and real estate, among others, have considerably larger weights in its portfolio compared to the S&P 500 ETF. At the same time, information technology and communication have a meaningfully lower influence on its returns.

And these differences in sector allocations have a profound impact on EUSA's factor story.

EUSA vs. IVV: Factor Exposures

Looking at the parameters in the table below, one main conclusion can be drawn: EUSA's principal advantage over IVV is its valuation (and lower beta, but only to a degree), while on other fronts, it is far behind. Is a cheaper valuation enough for a Buy thesis? In this particular case, it is clearly not.

Size And Value

Although we have become somewhat accustomed to the S&P 500 ETF's weighted average market capitalization well above $1 trillion, EUSA provides a nice reminder that this is just the consequence of its overallocation to the Magnificent Seven, and a WA market cap of an equally weighted portfolio is about 11.45x lower, as the trillion-dollar league accounts for only 1.75% of it.

Overall, the value factor is where EUSA is confidently ahead of IVV. For instance, my calculations show that it has a 24.3x adjusted weighted average LTM P/E ratio (a 4.11% adjusted WA EY) vs. IVV's 28.5x. Adjusted P/S and EV/EBITDA, plus its allocation to stocks with a B- Quant Valuation grade or higher, tell the exact same story.

Growth And Momentum

The EUSA portfolio's exposure to the growth and momentum factors is not particularly impressive, well below that of IVV, which is expectable assuming its lower allocation to communication and IT and especially bellwethers like NVIDIA Corporation (NVDA).

Quality

EUSA lags IVV on the quality front as well, with all the selected metrics from the WA capital efficiency indicators, like Return on Assets to the allocation to stocks with a B- Quant Profitability grade or higher, being notably weaker than the S&P 500 ETF's.

Low Volatility

Here, EUSA has a slight advantage over IVV, as its weighted average 24-month beta is at 0.91. However, the 60-month beta tells a different story, as it is marginally higher than 1, so I would not say that EUSA has significant exposure to the low volatility factor.

EUSA Performance: Strangely Higher Risk, Lower Reward

I believe it is intuitively evident that equal weighting is in most cases supportive of a lower beta, and this, in turn, is synonymous with slightly more comfortable downside capture (assuming the S&P 500 ETF is the market proxy). However, EUSA delivers quite a surprise: not only are its returns rather soft, but also its risk profile is weaker than IVV's.

First, let us look at its performance since the beginning of the year. I find it pertinent to add the Goldman Sachs Equal Weight U.S. Large Cap Equity ETF (GSEW) and the Invesco S&P 500® Equal Weight ETF (RSP) to the analysis given their similar premises.

So what we see is rather expectable, as EUSA's value tilt has hindered it from capturing enough of the S&P 500's upside this year. What is interesting and fairly disappointing, though, is that EUSA has not only underperformed IVV but also its peers GSEW and RSP.

Yet this is just the tip of the iceberg, so let us look at the September 2015–September 2025 period.

Here, EUSA underperformed IVV by 3.83% in annualized return. For context, its annual active return (with IVV used as a benchmark) since the strategy change was above zero just twice: in 2016 and 2022.

However, a bigger problem that I see here is that it delivered a meaningfully higher standard deviation, a deeper maximum drawdown, and a downside capture ratio above 113%, a surprisingly poor result for a portfolio with stronger value characteristics than those of the S&P 500 ETF.

I would also like to compare its results to GSEW's and RSP's, but over a shorter period, October 2017–September 2025, as GSEW was incepted in September 2017.

What we see this time is even more disappointing, as EUSA not only trailed IVV but also both peers, delivering the weakest risk-adjusted returns in the group (the Sharpe and Sortino ratios) and the highest downside capture ratio. RSP still had a deeper maximum drawdown, though.

Final Thoughts

To conclude, the essential takeaway of my analysis today is that investors who are seeking more comfortable valuations and are not much concerned with the growth factor should find EUSA mostly matching their standards. However, if they are anticipating an impending valuation reset in this high-priced market and thus are looking for a more comfortable risk profile, they should understand that EUSA sadly does not offer much here. Surprisingly, it has been delivering not only weaker total returns but also higher volatility and deeper maximum drawdowns.

So while I find the S&P 500's stretched valuations and overallocation to just a handful of the top tech names concerning, EUSA does not make it to my Buy list.

data.title: Cosan: End Of An Empire
data.description:
Introduction

Cosan (NYSE:CSAN) IPOed in 2005; at the time, I was working at JPMorgan (JPM) and was assigned the task of covering the company. My initial analysis was a sell rating, and the report was to be titled: Tulips, Internet, and now Ethanol, alluding to various bubbles of the past. Unfortunately, I was convinced to change the Sell to a Hold with a warning to investors of unsustainable ethanol prices. Fast forward 20 years, and Cosan is now on the verge of extinction, after accumulating a dozen companies through that time, touting its vision and execution, it is the legacy ethanol business that finally led to its demise.

What is Cosan

Today, Cosan is a holding with a portfolio of five companies, two of which are listed on the Brazilian market. It owned a minority (6%) stake in Vale (VALE) for a few years without success in influencing its growth and development.

  • Raizen (44%) was the legacy sugarcane and ethanol business that changed its name when it merged with Shell’s (SHEL) gasoline distribution business in Brazil. Ethanol is used as an oil substitute, and cars are built with the capacity to use both fuels (flexfuel). Additionally, 30% of ethanol is mixed into gasoline. Thus, ethanol prices move with oil, while the refined sugar business, which comes out of the same plant, has its own supply and demand factors; Raizen is inherently a commodity business with low-risk fuel distribution. Cosan will not buy into a capital increase and should reduce its stake, essentially walking away from its legacy business.
  • Compass (88%) holds the natural gas distribution business, Comgas in Sao Paulo, and 11 other regional distributors in JV with Mitsui. This has been the most successful acquisition, and Cosan, post capital increase, should eventually become 100% Compass. The private equity groups that are taking over Cosan (with shareholder agreements) own natural gas production assets that make clear sense to merge sometime in the future.
  • Moove (70%) was acquired from Exxon and is the lubricant manufacturer (motor oil) and sells Mobile brands in Brazil and Latin America. A relatively stable and profitable business, except when the plant blew up, which caused a disruption in production and incurred over US$200m in added capex to rebuild this year.
  • Rumo (30%) is the largest railroad operator in Brazil, with a focus on transporting agricultural products (soy, corn) from the interior to the ports. It's a capital-intensive business as it expands its rail networks, and it is also price/margin sensitive depending on crop volumes (the weather) to fill capacity while competing with trucking. Cosan should sell down its stake over time.
  • Radar (30%) is an agricultural landowner with over 300k hectares spread across 8 Brazilian states. This is worth about US$700m and is slated for sale.

Private Equity to the Rescue

Cosan is in the midst of raising R$10bn (US$1.8bn), with R$7.2bn coming from private equity (BTG partners and Perfin) and $750m from the current majority owners' family office. This means that the Ommeto family will reduce its stake from 36% to 21%, and private equity will own 39%, with the market around 40%. All of the new cash will be used to reduce debt, and it will not support any of the companies in its portfolio; in fact, it will be focused on winding down, selling all but Compass, essentially ending the empire that once was.

Valuation

The capital increase is priced at R$5 or US$3.7 per ADR, which is about a 15% discount to current prices. However, this is 100% dilution; the share count will more than double, and while all of this cash will be used to lower debt (buy bonds), the valuation post-money is still not attractive in my view.

As a holding company, its cash flow is derived from the dividends received from its five companies, which, according to a consensus, is slated to drop in 2026 since 2025 included several extraordinary payouts from Compass and Raizen.

The FCF I calculated assumes a modest reduction in SGA, which is mostly managing its debt. The primary cash flow gain comes from lower financial expenses as Cosan pays down R$10bn in 2026, while the remaining debt should see lower rates as Brazil enters a rate cut cycle (current Selic, or benchmark rates, are 15%).

This means that post-money, an EV/FCF for 2026 is high at 56x with a 2% FCF yield, which indicates that the current and even the capital increase price is optimistic. I don’t see upside until 2027, when Compass increases dividends and Moove gets past its high capex post-accident. One key positive is if Radar sells its land bank, but this is a minor cash event given the 30% stake.

Many investors may value Cosan on an NAV and sum of the parts basis, which I find demands substantially more guesswork. This requires assuming market values for most of the portfolio and then assigning a holding company discount. According to some reports, the post-money discount is around 20%, which I would argue is not sufficient.

Risk

The main risk to the sell rating is if Brazil's interest rates fall to lower levels, which boosts cash flow and valuation. If Cosan can sell down stakes in Rumo and Moove at good prices and de-lever faster is another risk. Finally, as a de facto arm of the BTG, the group's capacity for value creation may benefit Cosan in yet unforeseen ways.

Conclusion

I rate Cosan a sell. Despite the share price collapse and now apparent white knight to the rescue, it seems to me the post-capital increase valuation is not compelling and may suffer further headwinds as the company’s new shareholders attempt to unwind the holding. This is a multi-year process that may keep Cosan shares stagnant.

data.title: YTSL:CA: High Yield, Higher Risk - Why The Math Doesn't Work
data.description:
The Tesla (TSLA) Yield Shares Purpose ETF (NEOE:YTSL:CA) is a leveraged covered call strategy on Tesla (TSLA) for the Canadian markets. With covered calls of up to 50% of underlying Tesla holdings and a moderate leverage of ~25%, YTSL:CA aims to generate income while riding a bull thesis on Tesla.

Volatility of the Underlying

As I have written in the past, for income investors looking for market-agnostic strategies, single-stock covered call strategies often require a bullish thesis and do not necessarily benefit from very high volatility levels. Tesla lands somewhere below MARA in terms of volatility levels in general, indicating its suitability for an option strategy. That is, there is a likely balance between too high volatility to build reliable and consistent option income around and too low to generate sufficient premiums.

Plus Leverage

If volatility levels of the underlying look okay, the leverage adds an added layer of complexity, magnifying the moves by 25% in the case of YTSL:CA. The leverage could push losses and gains by ~25%, making the total returns profile volatile, but should not impact the setup's ability to generate income because of underlying volatility.

In short, a leveraged underlying position with an option layer on top is not theoretically the end of a Buy thesis in YTSL:CA. Although given the total return volatility, I usually prefer a less volatile underlying like Google or a broader index when it comes to leveraged holdings - more to do with concentrated bets versus diversification and if a concentrated bet then a more reliable, low-volatility single stock.

Bull Thesis Requirement

The overall structure of YTSL:CA spells caution not directly because of the underlying's volatility or because of the leverage. The reason why YTSL:CA becomes difficult to ride is the requirement of the bull thesis on the underlying where the multiple risks are hinging on (with YTSL:CA's currency hedges removing even currency fluctuation factors from the mix). And there I find Tesla falling short. You don't need to disagree with my call on Tesla - just the fact that a broad market index is resilient by definition (replacing non-performers with fresh blood over time), makes it a better choice to risk my capital on for income. I can accept the leverage and volatility risks on the S&P 500 or the S&P/TSX 60 in the long run, but when it comes to a single stock, the ask of the underlying's long-term bull thesis becomes paramount. Tesla is a polarizing stock - not the stock you can buy and forget. Just a reminder on those who believe in its reward potential—income plans underlying stocks should not necessarily be about growth as much as risk mitigation.

Given above is the total return profile of Tesla versus YTSL:CA. It is extremely clear that while there are some covered call strategy based ETFs that cap upside and provide better drawdown cushions - YTSL:CA's structure implies it becomes a leveraged version of Tesla. YTSL:CA merely magnifies Tesla's journey.

The ~88% rally in Tesla from the tariff sell-off in April 2025, is magnified by ~18% by YTSL:CA. On the way down, from December 2024 to April 2025, YTSL:CA does magnify the losses by only ~10%. That broadly means the strategy does seem to cushion drawdowns slightly better and capture upsides better too, but overall requires Tesla to grow to fund payouts. Theoretically, a covered call strategy can fund payouts simply from premiums earned, even if the underlying stays flat or even corrects slightly. YTSL:CA cannot do that. Its leveraged setup makes a bullish thesis in Tesla mandatory.

Redundant with Leveraged Tesla Plays

The performance of YTSL:CA, i.e. magnifying drawdowns less aggressively than upsides, but still magnifying it nonetheless, makes its return trajectory very similar to Tesla or a 25% leveraged Tesla position most of the time. There are times when YTSL:CA traces TSLA very closely, like in the period till March 2025 in the chart below, and then turns towards 1.25x Tesla from there on. I find the offerings of YTSL:CA quite redundant in that sense - where either Tesla or a leveraged Tesla ETF or a combination will suffice.

The payouts are clearly not much dependent on the option layer, as seen in the total return profile staying close to TSLA itself. So, YTSL:CA's proposition becomes even more redundant in my view - huffing and puffing with options for a ~23% yield, which is anyway funded by the underlying's growth more than anything else. That can easily be done simply by investing in Tesla or a leveraged Tesla ETF and paying out income in a DIY-style income setup.

Another important thing to note is that while the high yields could be lucrative to look at, unless the underlying grows at ~20% annually (setting aside 2-3% from options, which is still a tall ask in most cases), the fund is actually paying you back your money rather than growing it to fund income. That leads to NAV erosion over time. YTSL:CA may have been blessed by a growth in Tesla keeping with the ~20% ask long term so far, but a continued growth of those proportions will be required going ahead. Even if you are a believer, would it not make more sense to simply buy and hold Tesla and pay out income from it, instead of the complications and rigidity of YTSL:CA?

Unreliable Performance Too

While we saw ~18% higher total returns in the recent rally and ~10% higher drawdowns before that, this behavior has not been the predictable norm. Since inception, through the various ups and downs of Tesla, YTSL:CA has actually underperformed Tesla, while not even reducing the drawdowns as a benefit - which means the theory of simply amplifying returns on either side and lesser on the lower side is not necessarily true always.

Since option strategy-based ETFs are not fully transparent in minor strategy changes, and even the same strategy's implementation specifics could mean differing performances in similar market conditions, the performance cannot be labeled as "predictable". The current portfolio constitution shows around 20 call options written across various strikes - 23% out of the money and the remaining at the money or close. By simply altering this split, the amount of coverage of the portfolio in notional terms, underlying leverage specifics, or even changing strategies to include puts could alter the return expectations greatly.

YTSL:CA should be treated very much like an active strategy that still ends up poorer than Tesla, unless Tesla rallies. You may see several market conditions where YTSL:CA even underwhelms on its own promise of magnifying TSLA returns and funding payouts from it.

All in all, from the fact that it looks redundant, to its inability to consistently outperform the underlying (either in capturing upsides or reducing drawdowns), YTSL:CA is a Sell as far as I am concerned. It does not help that I do not have a long-term bullish view on Tesla. There could be minor tax efficiencies in theory, but a DIY portfolio could also harvest losses and manage its own tax implications. Most importantly, the options layer does not have a demonstrated income-generating track record over time to ensure the payout profile is any different from simply selling Tesla stocks and generating income i.e., capital gains as income.

data.title: Miami International: Unique Tech With Visible Growth Catalysts
data.description:
Investment action

I give a buy rating for Miami International Holdings (NYSE:MIAX) as I believe the market is underappreciating its market position and growth potential. To me, it is clear that MIAX has built a solid business with its proprietary technology and has won market share against much larger peers. With several near-term catalysts, such as the launched trading floor, the Bloomberg B500 partnership, and growing ODTE adoption, I expect it to continue growing robustly. Valuation should rerate higher as investors appreciate this.

Company description

MIAX is an exchange operator for multiple asset classes across geographies. It has its own proprietary trading platform (built entirely in-house) that covers regulated exchanges, including options, equities, and futures. About 90% of revenue comes from its four automated US options exchanges. Beyond options, MIAX also operates a US equities exchange contributing ~3% of revenue, a vertically integrated futures business, which is ~6% of revenue, and international listings for the remaining 1%.

The Options Market

MIAX has managed to carve out a nice position in the US options market. For a brief history of the industry, it has grown massively in size in recent years, with activities surging since covid as investors look to hedge their positions, and when zero-commission trading opened the door for retail investors to participate more aggressively. New products like zero-day-to-expiry [ODTE] also increased the frequency of trades.

What sets MIAX apart, in my view, is its tech platform. As said above, the MIAX Exchange trading system was built entirely in-house, and because of that, MIAX is able to maximize throughput, latency, reliability, and determinism. These are the key performance attributes that matter most to institutional traders and market makers. Let me explain each term for better understanding.

  1. Throughput measures how many messages (like quotes, orders, and cancellations) the system can process per second. Here, MIAX Options, for instance, is able to sustain up to 32 million messages per second, providing ample capacity to accommodate 6-sigma-like events (i.e., extreme volatile trading sessions). The last thing you want as a trader or market maker is for orders to not be able to come through.
  2. Latency is the time required to process and acknowledge an order, which averages between 14 and 18 microseconds (per the S-1). Low latency is important for liquidity providers and high-frequency traders who rely on execution speed to make profits. For those that don’t trade as frequently, think about when you played an online game; a longer latency could mess up your entire gameplay. In the case of a trader, imagine the potential losses if you are dealing with millions of orders.
  3. Lastly, reliability is perhaps the most important attribute. MIAX has achieved 99.999% uptime since its inception, with no material software failures. This is essential, especially when markets are volatile. Users need to know the exchange will perform consistently; if not, they will be exposed to huge risk when they need to liquidate positions.

While larger exchange groups have scale, the reality is they also have a lot more areas to invest in and are not able to allocate all their resources to make the best option product. This leaves room for a more focused operator like MIAX to win share. And this has played out, with numbers to back it up. Since launching its first exchange in 2012, MIAX has increased its share from 0% to ~16% in 1H 2025. Evidently, MIAX has proven it can win share, and I expect this momentum to continue as it is unlikely that large incumbents are going to rip out their tech infrastructure to rebuild everything from the bottom up to match MIAX capabilities. There is just too much disruption risk.

Growth catalysts

There are multiple growth catalysts in place. The most immediate growth catalyst is the launch of the MIAX Sapphire physical trading floor in Miami. This should enable MIAX to capture the ~6% of multi-list options volume that still trades on physical floors (the complex order types), which are more profitable than standard electronic flows. And with this, MIAX’s offerings would be more holistic. Beyond the near term, catalysts include the adoption of single-name ODTE options (liquidity for single-name ODTE is still low) and the expansion toward 24/5 trading hours. Both of these would materially increase trading activity, which is music to MIAX’s ears.

Another key growth driver is MIAX’s exclusive, long-term partnership with Bloomberg, which grants it rights to list proprietary derivatives based on Bloomberg’s equity indices. The initial rollout is for the B500 index, with the next introduction of B500 futures targeted for Q4 2025/Q1 2026; cash-settled options targeted for 1H 2026; and options on futures targeted for 2H 2026. The challenge will be in building the B500’s credibility as a viable alternative, but in my view, there are two main reasons the B500 has a real chance of gaining traction.

First, Bloomberg is a reputable product with very strong adoption in the finance world, and as such, the B500 has instant institutional legitimacy. Moreover, Bloomberg indices are already embedded in ETFs and structured products (mentioned on Bloomberg’s website), and that naturally makes adoption of the B500 a logical move rather than a massive change. Second, the B500’s benchmarks may offer a fresh alternative to the S&P 500, which is exclusively licensed to CBOE. Even small differences in how companies are weighted or selected can give traders new ways to hedge or find pricing gaps between the two benchmarks.

If successful, the B500 could evolve into a credible alternative benchmark, which is timely given investors are increasingly questioning the concentration risk of the S&P 500.

Valuation

There are data points that show that the entire options market is growing rapidly, like this note from CBOE and Moomoo, so I am not worried that MIAX will see demand fall off the cliff anytime soon. In fact, when the S&P 500 is continuously making new highs, it should attract more liquidity and also hedging occasions.

Say that MIAX can continue to grow revenue by mid-teens, which shouldn’t be hard since it still has less than 20% market share, the entire market size is growing, and it has visible growth catalysts. This should bring its revenue close to ~$500 million by FY2028. There are a few players that are good comps to MIAX: CME Group, Nasdaq, Intercontinental Exchange, and CBOE Global Markets. All of them are established incumbents and trade at a premium vs. MIAX. On average, they trade at ~10x forward revenue with high-single-digit growth. With my belief that MIAX should grow in the mid-teens, which is a premium to MIAX, I’d say MIAX deserves to trade in the same range. But given the lower profitability and scale, I haircut 1 multiple turn and value MIAX at 9x.

Risk

Growth could be much slower than I modelled if MIAX faces delays in rolling out its new index products or if adoption is poorer than expected. This would delay monetization and weaken the overall growth story. While MIAX has gained share, incumbents could decide to pour significant capital into this area if the unit economics get a lot better. They may not be at the same level in technical terms, but given their distribution and scale, they could still hurt MIAX’s pricing power. In addition, a sizable drop in volatility or regulatory shifts affecting 0DTE could weigh on trading activity.

Conclusion

My rating for MIAX is buy. Its technological edge and market share gains show that it is able to win demand. Coupled with that with the growth catalysts in place, I see a strong growth story ahead. Moreover, valuation is not expensive when compared to incumbents with lower growth. A re-rating should happen if MIAX can continue to show robust growth in the coming years.

data.title: WTI Oil: Crude Rallies Above $60 On Fresh U.S. Sanctions And U.S. Million Barrel Purchase
data.description:
By Christian Norman

Finding support at 6-month lows of around $56.40 per barrel, WTI has rallied just shy of 8.6% in the last three sessions alone. Currently trading at $62.04, up 4.10% in yesterday’s session, recent performance marks the best three-day stint since late July.

As ever, let’s take a look at some of the macro themes at play, followed by some technical analysis as we attempt to answer the immortal question: what’s next for WTI?

WTI (West Texas Intermediate): Key takeaways 24/10/2025

  • Gapping up 1.1% at Thursday’s open, WTI crude has found renewed buying support on new sanction announcements from the United States on leading Russian oil companies, including Rosneft and Lukoil
  • Constrained previously by logistical problems of safe storage, and of course, funding issues, the US Department of Energy confirmed on Tuesday its intentions to purchase 1 million barrels of crude oil to replenish reserves, bolstering crude gains
  • Otherwise, and in the bigger picture, a cloud of oversupply fears lingers over crude oil markets, especially considering record output from non-OPEC+ members

WTI (West Texas Intermediate): Where were we?

It’s high time I returned to my commentary roots and wrote some more coverage on crude oil markets. Although it would be fair to say that oil has played second fiddle to precious metals in recent months, both in terms of market interest and, indeed, performance.

With that said, this appears to be changing, with recent geopolitical developments offering some welcome upside and, crucially, boosting WTI pricing above $60 for the first time since earlier this month.

Without further ado, let’s break down some major macroeconomic themes and conclude with some market technicals, including some price targets.

WTI (WTICOUSD): Fundamental Analysis 24/10/2025

New US sanctions on Russian oil: Reported Wednesday, the United States announced new sanctions on Russian oil exports, following an apparent breakdown in ceasefire negotiations between Russia and Ukraine.

Read the full press release here

Coming only one day after Trump shelved a planned meeting in Budapest with Putin, it would seem that frustrations are running high after the demands of an immediate ceasefire have fallen on deaf ears.

"I don’t want to have a wasted meeting. I don’t want to have a waste of time, so I’ll see what happens. We did all of these great deals, great peace deals, they’re all peace deals. Agreements, solid agreements every one of them"

President Donald Trump, speaking to reporters at the White House, 21/10/2025

Clearly, the sanctions are intended as a bargaining tool to help encourage a peace deal, but at least so far, words from the Kremlin suggest that the Russian domestic oil industry will remain largely unaffected, boasting of its level of immunity from Western sanctions.

While Trump hopes the economic impact of new sanctions will encourage Putin to return to the negotiating table, only time will tell how effective these measures will be.

As for oil pricing, the associated fallout raises questions about supply, which is a positive development for pricing. This holds even more true when considering that the market narrative has been almost exclusively one of oversupply - the most recent geopolitical developments question this assumption somewhat, especially if tensions escalate.

US to buy 1 million crude barrels to replenish reserves: I think most would agree that, especially when compared to previous presidents, Trump shares a very particular relationship with the crude oil markets. After all, who could forget this legendary catchphrase?

If put simply, however, Trump's policy surrounding crude oil centers almost exclusively revolves around two core tenets:

  1. To maintain America’s lead as the #1 producer of crude oil worldwide, therefore establishing control over supply
  2. To keep oil prices low to promote economic growth

On this basis, the latest development is the intent to purchase 1 million barrels of crude oil to replenish the Strategic Petroleum Reserve (SPR), which recently saw record levels of depletion under the administration of former President Joe Biden.

Being previously constrained by logistical matters and, of course, funding, the EIA will undoubtedly want to capitalise on historically low crude oil prices to increase its stockpile. Naturally, markets have interpreted this information as positive for crude oil, helping to boost pricing.

Non-OPEC+ members report record crude output: While not as contemporaneous as the other two themes, a significant macro headwind continues to dictate the direction of crude markets: the fear of oversupply.

Most recently, this is reflected in record output from non-OPEC countries, which have contributed to the current bearish bias. Otherwise, the aforementioned EIA has also confirmed sustained high levels of production.

While there have been some attempts to stagger output increases by OPEC, they have proved limited in effectiveness, with crude oil inventories rising globally.

In a nutshell, although the previous two themes are bullish for crude oil, the longer-term bias likely remains bearish from a supply standpoint.

WTI (WTICOUSD): Technical Analysis 24/10/2025

While recent upside has been impressive, rallying by almost 8.6%, there is still plenty of work to be done if oil is to break the current downtrend.

While on a macro level, the narrative surrounding supply would have to change significantly to support this, technically, here’s some level to watch to the upside:

Price targets and support/resistance levels:

  • Price target #1 - Previous support turned resistance - $62.564
  • Price target #2 - $63.564 - 61.8% Fib

What is encouraging, however, is that one of my personal favourite indicators, the SSL channel, has flipped to report a bullish bias. While this can occur during periods of market consolidation, when combined with a rising OBV volume, it may perhaps signify that a larger change is afoot.

With that said, price action remains overwhelmingly bearish, assuming crude cannot break above ~$63.564, which might shake things up somewhat.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Massive Outlier In Owner's Equivalent Of Rent Pushed Down CPI, Core CPI, Core Services CPI: Something Went Awry At The BLS
data.description:
OER weighs 26% of CPI, 33% of core CPI, 44% of core services CPI. It moves the needle. CPI inflation would have been a lot hotter without this outlier situation.

The delayed release of the Consumer Price Index today, cobbled together with perhaps not all the staff and means that the Bureau of Labor Statistics has normally available due to the government shutdown, was perhaps the best that could be done under the circumstances.

But there were a few things that were off, the most important of which was Owner’s Equivalent of Rent (OER), a huge component in CPI, accounting for 26% of overall CPI, for 33% of core CPI, and for 44% of core services CPI; it was a massive historic outlier.

OER rose by only 0.13% in September from August (blue line in the chart), according to the BLS today, compared to 0.38% in the prior month, and compared to the 12-month range between +0.27% (May) and +0.41% (July). Something went wrong there, and given its huge weight, OER significantly pushed down the month-to-month readings of overall CPI, core CPI, and core services CPI.

If this situation with OER hadn’t happened, the inflation readings today would have been a lot hotter than they were, particularly core services CPI, where OER weighs 44% and core CPI where OER weighs 33%.

OER is not a measure of rent. The measure of rent is the Rent CPI. OER is a stand-in for the costs of homeownership. OER indirectly reflects the expenses of homeownership, such as homeowners’ insurance, HOA fees, property taxes, and maintenance.

It’s the only measure for those expenses in the CPI. It is based on what a large group of homeowners estimates their home would rent for, with the assumption that homeowners would try to recoup their cost increases by raising the rent.

Overall CPI rose by 0.31% (+3.8% annualized) in September from August. So not a benign inflation reading, but the second worst since January. And yet it was pushed down by the OER outlier situation (blue in the chart below).

Year-over-year, overall CPI rose by 3.01%, the worst increase since May 2024, and the sixth acceleration in a row. It would have risen more without the OER outlier (red).

“Core” CPI, which excludes food and energy components to track underlying inflation, clearly showed the massive push-down from the OER outlier. It rose by only 0.23% (2.8% annualized) in September from August (blue) which caused the year-over-year increase to decelerate to +3.0% (red).

Core services CPI, where OER weighs about 44%, was heavily impacted by the OER outlier; it rose by only 0.24% in September from August (+2.9% annualized).

Core services are about 60% of the overall CPI. They include many of the essentials that consumers cannot do without – housing (OER!), medical care, health insurance, auto insurance, tenant’s insurance, subscriptions; telephone, internet, and wireless services; lodging, rental cars, airline fares, education, movies, sports events, club memberships, water, sewer, trash collection, motor vehicle maintenance and repair, etc.

But with 44% of it gone haywire in some way, it doesn’t really indicate anything for September.

The CPI for durable goods dipped in September by 0.13%, and year-over-year, decelerated to +1.8%.

Major durable goods categories MoM YoY
Durable goods overall -0.1% 1.8%
New vehicles 0.2% 0.8%
Used vehicles -0.4% 5.1%
Household furnishings (furniture, appliances, floor coverings, tools) 0.2% 3.0%
Sporting goods (bicycles, equipment, etc.) 1.0% 0.0%
Information technology (computers, smartphones, etc.) -0.8% -5.1%

The chart below shows the price level, not the percentage change, of the durable goods. The index had spiked by 25% during the pandemic, and then prices came down some from those highs, starting in mid-2022.

The index is dominated by new and used vehicles. Used vehicle prices had spiked by 55% from mid-2020 to mid-2022, then declined. But 13 months ago, in August last year, used vehicle prices started zigzagging higher again on tight supplies.

Given how messed up the core services CPI is due to the outlier situation of OER, it doesn’t make much sense to delve into services inflation further at this point.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Consumer Price Index: Inflation Rises To 3.0% In September, Lower Than Expected
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Inflation heated up for a fifth straight month in September as the headline figure for the Consumer Price Index rose to 3.0% year-over-year. This is up from 2.9% in August but lower than the expected 3.1% reading.

To two decimal places (3.01%), this is the highest level since May 2024. On a monthly basis, prices rose 0.3%, down from the 0.4% growth in August and less than the expected 0.4% monthly increase.

Additionally, the core CPI was at 3.0% year-over-year, down from 3.1% in August and lower than the forecast of 3.1%. On a monthly basis, core prices rose 0.2%, down from the 0.3% growth in August and less than the expected 0.3% monthly increase.

Here is the introduction from the BLS summary, which leads with the seasonally adjusted monthly data:

The Consumer Price Index for All Urban Consumers (CPI-U) increased 0.3 percent on a seasonally adjusted basis in September, after rising 0.4 percent in August, the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics reported today. Over the last 12 months, the all items index increased 3.0 percent before seasonal adjustment. Note that September CPI data collection was completed before the lapse in appropriations.

The index for gasoline rose 4.1 percent in September and was the largest factor in the all items monthly increase, as the index for energy rose 1.5 percent over the month. The food index increased 0.2 percent over the month as the food at home index rose 0.3 percent and the food away from home index increased 0.1 percent.

The index for all items less food and energy rose 0.2 percent in September, after rising 0.3 percent in each of the 2 preceding months. Indexes that increased over the month include shelter, airline fares, recreation, household furnishings and operations, and apparel. The indexes for motor vehicle insurance, used cars and trucks, and communication were among the few major indexes that decreased in September.

The all items index rose 3.0 percent for the 12 months ending September, after rising 2.9 percent over the 12 months ending August. The all items less food and energy index also rose 3.0 percent over the last 12 months. The energy index increased 2.8 percent for the 12 months ending September. The food index increased 3.1 percent over the last year.

The first chart is an overlay of headline CPI and core CPI (excludes Food and Energy) since the turn of the century. The highlighted two percent level is the Federal Reserve's target inflation rate.

In September, prices accelerated from 2.92% in August to 3.01%, the highest level since May 2024. Meanwhile, core prices slowed from 3.11% in August to 3.02% in September.

The next chart shows both series since 1957, the year the government first began tracking core inflation. In recent years, we have seen some of the highest inflation rates since the second of the two recessions in the early 1980s.

However, inflation has slowly made its way back down but has proven sticky. Core CPI is currently sitting at a level last seen in the early 1990s, while headline CPI is near levels seen in 2017-18.

Consumer Price Index Components

The Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) divides all expenditures into eight categories and assigns a relative size to each, which is shown in the chart below. The BLS weighs these annually, with the latest weighting taking place in December 2024.

Here is a table showing the annualized change in Headline and Core CPI, not seasonally adjusted, for each of the past six months. Also included are the eight components of Headline CPI and a separate entry for Energy, which is a collection of sub-indexes in Housing and Transportation.

We can make some inferences about how inflation is impacting our personal expenses depending on our relative exposure to the individual components. Some of us have higher transportation costs, others medical costs, etc. A conspicuous feature in the year-over-year table is the volatility in energy, significantly a result of gasoline prices, which is also reflected in Transportation.

Here is the same table as above with month-over-month numbers (not seasonally adjusted).

Note: For additional information on the component composition of the Consumer Price Index, see our Inside the Consumer Price Index.

Inflation: Fed's 2% Target

The Fed is on record as having a target rate of 2% for core inflation. The Fed traditionally uses the PCE Price Index as their preferred inflation gauge; however, the Consumer Price Index is another common measure.

The COVID-19 pandemic helped launch inflation into its highest levels since the 1980s. As a result, the Fed has been battling high inflation over the past few years with its monetary policy. Inflation has eased off of its 2022 highs; however, the back half of 2024 and most of 2025 has shown just how sticky it can be.

In their latest meeting, the Fed implemented a widely anticipated 25 basis point cut to the federal funds rate (FFR), bringing it to a range of 4.00%-4.25%. This marked the first rate cut of the year, with two more quarter-point cuts anticipated before year end.

The statement from the meeting revealed the committee believes "inflation has moved up and remains somewhat elevated," but that they are strongly committed to returning inflation to its 2% objective.

The Fed will meet again next week, where they are expected to resume cutting rates. However, the big question is how large of a cut it will be. At the time of writing, the CMEFedWatch Tool shows a 97% likelihood the Fed will cut rates by 25 basis points and a 3% likelihood they will hold rates steady.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Integer Holdings: Growth Standstill Comes As A Shock
data.description:
About a year ago, I concluded that shares of Integer Holdings Corporation (NYSE:ITGR) were more than re-rated at the time. The company saw solid share price returns at the time, backed up by resilient organic growth and accompanied by some margin expansion, but nonetheless, valuation multiples were still on the increase.

Given premium multiples applied to the business at the time, leverage taken on, and non-convincing bolt-on dealmaking taking place (both on the buy and sell side), I was leaning cautious.

Following a solid first half of the year, the company is now guiding for growth stagnation, lasting into 2026, causing shares to crater, as the warning (and extent thereof) is shocking investors here.

While this might very well provide long-term opportunities, some more conservative use of leverage and execution is required to gain some confidence in the 2027 rebound.

Medical Device Outsourcing Manufacturing

Integer Holdings is a large medical device contract development and manufacturing outsourcer. The company employs some 11,000 workers which operate across some 17 manufacturing centers, as well as 9 R&D centers.

These activities generate about $1.7 billion in annual sales, which are classified across three segments. The largest of these are cardio & vascular, responsible for just over half of sales. Cardiac rhythm management & neuromodulation makes up nearly 40% of sales, complemented by smaller other markets.

The company claims that it is a leading partner of choice because of its vertically integrated business model and capabilities, including design & development, rapid prototyping, complex components, innovative technologies and finished devices. The company claims product breadth, many patents on its name, and recurring revenues to create a balanced business, one with recurring revenues, driven by the fact that much of its sales are generated under multi-year agreements.

Following some divestments in recent years, the company has become a more focused business, including the divestment of AS&O, the portable medical exit, and divestment of Electrochem. Having obtained greater focus, the goal of the company is to really bolster its innovative capabilities and launch new products, as the company has been making some strategically focused acquisitions to make this happen as well.

Over the past decade, the company has more or less doubled sales to $1.7 billion and generally has seen operating margins come in quite stable in the low-teens. One small caveat to make is that some dilution has been incurred, with the share count increasing by about a quarter over this period of time.

A $50 stock a decade ago, ITGR hit the $100 mark around the time of the pandemic. It then saw real hiccups during the pandemic (and relate to that the havoc wrecked on supply chains). In the end, ITGR shares ended up rising to highs near the $150 mark at the start of the year, to now trade at half that level.

Revisiting The Thesis

After real headwinds seen during the pandemic, Integer reported stronger sales and margin recovery in 2023. Adjusted earnings were reported at $4.67 per share, as the gap with GAAP earnings was substantial at around two dollars, caused by about a dozen adjustments. Looking through these, including a large amortization charge, realistic earnings were seen around $4 per share.

The company guided for 2024 sales to increase to $1.75 billion, with EBITDA seen up to $365 million, badly needed based on a pro forma net debt load of $1.1 billion after a bolt-on M&A move early in the year. Pegging realistic earnings around $5 per share, but mindful of over 3 times leverage ratios, I did not immediately find appeal at $125 per share. This also came as the company announced the divestment of the Electrochem business at non-demanding (sales) multiples.

In the end, I concluded that the re-rating was complete. However, that was a bit premature, with shares ending up rising another twenty dollars to highs around $145 per share at the start of the year.

2025 - Reality Kicks In

ITGR began the year in the $140s as the company reported a 10% increase in 2024 sales to $1.72 billion, a touch light in comparison to the original guidance, although its 7% organic growth rates look quite decent.

Adjusted earnings were reported up some sixty-nine cents to $5.30 per share, as the gap to GAAP earnings of $3.40 per share actually was down a bit. However, adding back amortization charges, I saw realistic earnings close to $5 per share, all achieved on $361 million in EBITDA, all while net debt came in below the billion mark again.

The company guided for 2025 sales to move up to $1.85-$1.88 billion, with EBITDA seen around $411 million, with adjusted earnings seen just a few pennies above $6 per share. This however included the contribution of two pending acquisitions, that of Precision Coating and VSi Parylene, combined seen boosting 2025 sales by around $60 million. Cash expenses in relation to these deals were seen at $175 million, set to increase net debt over $1.1 billion again.

In March, a big financing action took place with $875 million in convertible notes priced, due in 2030. An attractive 1.875% coupon came at the expense of conversion (and potential dilution) with conversion seen just over $150 per share.

Following two resilient quarterly earnings numbers, the company basically maintained the full year sales and EBITDA guidance, yet boosted the adjusted earnings per share guidance to $6.25-$6.51 per share. In this light, many things looked a lot better.

With realistic earnings seen at $5 and change and shares gradually coming back to the $100 mark, realistic earnings multiples in the mid-twenties in the fall of 2024 had come down to multiples in the high-teens throughout this year.

A Bombshell

After posting 11% total revenue growth in these second quarter, Integer reported an 8% increase in third quarter sales, with organic revenue growth reported at 7%. However, the company trimmed the full year sales guidance to $1.84-$1.85 billion, with EBITDA seen at $401 million and adjusted earnings seen at $6.36 per share. Net debt is reported at $1.16 billion, this however including a lot of cheaper convertible debt, with leverage ratios seen just below 3 times here.

The real issue, besides the modest reduction in the 2025 guidance, is that the company now expects that 2026 will become a transition year, which comes completely unexpected. This includes (at a midpoint of) organic sales of just 2%, reported sales being flat (despite dealmaking efforts) with adjusted earnings being pretty flattish as well.

This comes really out of the blue and while the company sees growth returning in 2027, investors reacted very shocked, being completely surprised by the news. Still feeling comfortable to work with $5 in earnings per share as realistic earnings going forwards valuation have come in meaningfully given the pricing action.

With shares overnight falling from levels over the $100 mark to $73, valuation multiples have compressed. Based on the $5 realistic earnings number now, and also seen next year, multiples have overnight collapsed to 14–15 times, all because solid high-single-digit organic growth basically is coming to a standstill.

What Now?

The low 2026 guidance is a big negative surprise, although the re-rating has been very aggressive as well, likely due to the extent of the reset of expectations and some leverage employed.

Moreover, investors are digesting the fact that the timing of the shortfall comes after long-term CEO Jospeph Dziedzic just performed his latest earnings call for the business, with the uncertainty about the positioning creating another uncertain factor for investors to digest. His claim that the backlog was pretty stable in the low $700 millions, is not comforting investors, with the shortfall to the 2026 outlook really attributed to 3 rather aggressive and reported to be individual customer trends having changed.

In between all this, I am performing a balancing act. Having been impressed by the performance to date, the reset is very substantial, but so is the pullback in the share price, although the use of leverage is a bit high for my comfort.

However, I think that current ITRG levels do start to offer appeal, although real execution is required in the near term by a new CEO, while some leverage is taken on as well, all which makes me patiently await to get involved here.
